site.title: WorkComp Risk Radar
site.tagline: Daily risk signals across the NSW & Australian workers compensation insurance landscape.

digests.posture: Day three of the new NSW regime, and the freshest signals are the operational plumbing of the reform rather than its headline settings: the Industrial Relations Commission's new gatekeeping role now sits in front of every disputed bullying or harassment psychological-injury claim, two employer-excess exemptions (five-day notification and small-business) fell away at commencement, and NSW's annual indexation shift for exempt workers takes effect today — while interstate, WorkCover WA's revised permanent-impairment guidelines commenced 1 July as a comparator for NSW's move to a single WPI assessment.

Day two of the new regime, and the WHS side of the ledger now bites: from today section 26A makes NSW codes of practice — including the psychosocial-hazards code — enforceable benchmarks rather than guidance, sharpening employer and insurer prevention exposure just as workers-compensation entitlements narrow; the freshly operative 2026 Market Practice and Premium Guidelines also tighten the experience-rating cap, while Queensland and South Australia both hold their 2026-27 premium rates flat as cross-scheme sustainability comparators.

Commencement day: from 4pm today (30 June) and 1 July the NSW reform package moves from legislation to live operation, so the signal shifts from anticipating change to operating under it — new claims, premium and market-practice settings now bind insurers and agents, with late government concessions softening the day-one rules and a $344m prevention package coming online alongside.

Commencement eve in NSW: with the reform package taking effect from 4pm 30 June / 1 July, the live signals are a pre-commencement rush of claims lodged under the old rules, mounting evidence that tighter eligibility is pushing mental-injury claims into superannuation, and a step-change in psychosocial enforcement exposure.

NSW dominates the signal: the scheme's largest reform in over a decade commences 30 June – 1 July, bringing new SIRA guidelines, a frozen premium target, a new employer excess and a far tighter psychological-injury test.
